title: How Open Source Enables Collaboration in Creating a Platform
gist: At KubeCon Europe, two platform engineers from a bank (Paramonova and Cusin) argue that a platform is a collaboration system, not a feature set, and that engineers trust platforms through predictable behavior. Adopting open source in a conservative banking environment demanded a cultural shift toward transparency and shared ownership, not just new tools.

insights.claim: A platform is fundamentally a collaboration system where platform teams depend on application teams and both need shared standards, not merely a product with features.
insights.speaker: Ben Linders
insights.so_what: Reframes platform engineering as an organizational-trust problem, relevant to how enterprises should staff and measure internal platform investments.
insights.quote: A platform is a collaboration system
insights.locator: A platform is a collaboration system, Paramonova said

insights.claim: Developers trust a platform because it behaves predictably, not because of its features, and that trust is built by operating the platform consistently day after day.
insights.speaker: Ben Linders
insights.so_what: A durable principle: reliability and consistency, not feature velocity, drive internal-platform adoption—useful lens for evaluating dev-tooling vendors.
insights.quote: developers do not trust a platform because of its features; they trust it because it behaves predictably
insights.locator: A banking environment first requires trust

insights.claim: Rather than exposing every Kubernetes capability, the team provided sensible defaults and opinionated workflows so developers could focus on applications instead of infrastructure complexity.
insights.speaker: Ben Linders
insights.so_what: Confirms the 'golden path' abstraction as the winning platform strategy over raw Kubernetes exposure, a signal for platform-tooling design.
insights.quote: Instead of exposing every Kubernetes capability, we tried to provide sensible defaults and opinionated workflows.
insights.locator: Another important aspect was reducing cognitive load

insights.claim: Making platform responsibilities explicit—which services the platform team operates, expected service levels, and where team responsibilities begin—was central to building user trust.
insights.speaker: Ben Linders
insights.so_what: Clear ownership boundaries as a trust mechanism, a practical governance pattern for internal platforms.
insights.locator: We also made platform responsibilities explicit

themes.theme: Open source as culture, not tooling

insights.claim: Adopting open source in a bank was not obvious because of support and accountability concerns, and succeeding required changing how people think, collaborate, and build, not just swapping tools.
insights.speaker: Ben Linders
insights.so_what: Highlights the cultural barrier to open-source adoption in regulated industries—a friction point for open-source vendors selling into finance.
insights.quote: If you only change the tools, but not how people think, collaborate, and build, you will hit a wall.
insights.locator: Open source is not just a software choice

insights.claim: Adopting Kubernetes means joining a community with norms, expectations, and documented decision-making, which pulled the bank's engineering culture toward more transparency, shared ownership, and craft.
insights.speaker: Ben Linders
insights.so_what: Tool adoption as a Trojan horse for cultural change—an underappreciated second-order effect of open-source standardization in enterprises.
insights.quote: When you adopt Kubernetes, you are not just adopting a container orchestrator. You are joining a community with norms, expectations, and a way of working together.
insights.locator: Open source communities have a specific culture

insights.claim: Sharing unfinished components with visibility into what and why turned users into early testers and collaborators, proving more valuable than shipping a polished product.
insights.speaker: Ben Linders
insights.so_what: Argues transparency beats polish for building platform-user relationships, counter to release-announcement culture.
insights.quote: That transparency turned out to be more valuable than a polished product.

insights.claim: Giving engineers real decision-making authority (not just task execution) and running open 'Genius Bar' support sessions changed engagement and attracted infrastructure teams to collaborate.
insights.speaker: Ben Linders
insights.so_what: Concrete engagement mechanisms (open support sessions, real ownership) that platform teams can replicate to build community.
